description:
You are a monitoring specialist who has just been assigned to enable a new “high-cpu” alert in both the YAML rule file used by your alert manager and the TOML threshold file used by a companion daemon.  
All work must be done from the command line inside the home directory “/home/user”.  
Everything you need already exists; you only have to edit the two configuration files and then record what you did in a short, one-line log entry.

1. Directory layout that already exists  
   • /home/user/monitoring/conf/alerts.yaml  
   • /home/user/monitoring/conf/thresholds.toml  
   • /home/user/monitoring/logs/ (empty; write-able by you)

2. Required final state  
   2.1 /home/user/monitoring/conf/alerts.yaml must still begin with the existing header comment and the pre-defined “disk_space_low” alert that is already there, but under the in-file marker  
        # --- ADD NEW ALERTS BELOW THIS LINE ---  
        you must append a **single, completely new YAML alert block** that is indented exactly two spaces from the “alerts:” array indent and has this precise text (including spacing):  

          - name: high_cpu
            severity: critical
            expr: avg(rate(cpu_usage_seconds_total[5m])) > 0.85
            for: 2m
            labels:
              team: ops
            annotations:
              summary: High CPU usage on {{ $labels.instance }}
              description: CPU has been above 85% for more than 2 minutes.

        • The resulting file must therefore contain ONE new “- name: high_cpu” object, correctly aligned under the “alerts:” array defined earlier in the file.  
        • Do not disturb any other content.

   2.2 /home/user/monitoring/conf/thresholds.toml must retain its initial “[global]” table, but **after** the marker  
        # --- ADD ALERT THRESHOLDS BELOW THIS LINE ---  
        you must append exactly this TOML block (respect indentation and blank lines):  

        [alert.high_cpu]
        enabled = true
        threshold = 0.85
        window = "5m"

        • There should be one blank line before the new “[alert.high_cpu]” table (the same style the file already follows).  
        • No other edits are allowed.

   2.3 Create a plain-text log file /home/user/monitoring/logs/config_update.log if it does not yet exist.  
        Append exactly one new line to it (terminate the line with a Unix newline “\n”), **nothing before or after**:  

        DONE: high_cpu alert appended

3. The automated checker will verify the following:  
   • alerts.yaml contains the literal line “- name: high_cpu” with the subsequent 8 indented lines exactly as shown, positioned below the specified marker.  
   • thresholds.toml contains the literal line “[alert.high_cpu]” followed by the three key/value lines exactly as shown, positioned below the specified marker.  
   • /home/user/monitoring/logs/config_update.log exists and its final line is exactly “DONE: high_cpu alert appended”.  
   • No other files are touched or created anywhere outside /home/user/monitoring.

4. Constraints  
   • You have normal (non-root) permissions only.  
   • Accomplish all three edits with a single terminal command of your choice (you may combine utilities via &&, ;, or a subshell).  
   • Do not launch a full-screen interactive editor such as vim or nano; the grading environment will not allow it.  
   • Command output to stdout/stderr is fine and will be ignored by the checker; only on-disk content matters.

Once these three conditions are met exactly, the task is complete.
truth:
Initial file states provided inside the container before the agent starts:

1. /home/user/monitoring/conf/alerts.yaml

# Managed by monitoring team
alerts:
  - name: disk_space_low
    severity: warning
    expr: (node_filesystem_free_bytes / node_filesystem_size_bytes) < 0.1
    for: 5m
    labels:
      team: ops
    annotations:
      summary: Low disk space on {{ $labels.instance }}
      description: Free disk space is below 10% for more than 5 minutes.

  # --- ADD NEW ALERTS BELOW THIS LINE ---

2. /home/user/monitoring/conf/thresholds.toml

[global]
timezone = "UTC"

# --- ADD ALERT THRESHOLDS BELOW THIS LINE ---

3. /home/user/monitoring/logs/ exists and is empty.

Expected exact final contents after correct completion:

1. /home/user/monitoring/conf/alerts.yaml

# Managed by monitoring team
alerts:
  - name: disk_space_low
    severity: warning
    expr: (node_filesystem_free_bytes / node_filesystem_size_bytes) < 0.1
    for: 5m
    labels:
      team: ops
    annotations:
      summary: Low disk space on {{ $labels.instance }}
      description: Free disk space is below 10% for more than 5 minutes.

  # --- ADD NEW ALERTS BELOW THIS LINE ---
  - name: high_cpu
    severity: critical
    expr: avg(rate(cpu_usage_seconds_total[5m])) > 0.85
    for: 2m
    labels:
      team: ops
    annotations:
      summary: High CPU usage on {{ $labels.instance }}
      description: CPU has been above 85% for more than 2 minutes.

2. /home/user/monitoring/conf/thresholds.toml

[global]
timezone = "UTC"

# --- ADD ALERT THRESHOLDS BELOW THIS LINE ---

[alert.high_cpu]
enabled = true
threshold = 0.85
window = "5m"

3. /home/user/monitoring/logs/config_update.log

DONE: high_cpu alert appended

The automated tests will:
• open alerts.yaml and confirm the contiguous 9-line “high_cpu” block appears exactly as above following the marker.
• open thresholds.toml and confirm the contiguous 4-line “[alert.high_cpu]” block appears exactly as above following the marker.
• open config_update.log, read its last line, and confirm it matches exactly “DONE: high_cpu alert appended”.
